triage_context.environment: Library / SDK (caller is the trust boundary)

findings.title: isEqual cycle-detection O(depth) scan -> O(n^2) on deeply nested structures now reachable after the stack-overflow fix
preconditions: The application must actually call _.isEqual(a, b) on attacker-influenced data (no automatic execution; the developer chooses to invoke it).
At least one operand must be attacker-controlled (e.g. parsed JSON from a request body).
The attacker-supplied operand must be a deeply-nested structure (deep linear chain of objects/arrays); flat/shallow inputs do not trigger the quadratic path.
Both operands must be structurally matching at each depth so the aStack accumulates rather than early-returning, and the depth must be large enough for n^2 CPU to be a meaningful DoS.
findings.rationale:
TRUE_POSITIVE by 3/3 verifiers. The per-node linear scan of aStack at isEqual.js:118-128 costs k at depth k; aStack holds the current traversal path (pushed at 133-134, unwound via the `true` sentinel at 29-33/136), so for a depth-n linear chain the scan sums to 1+2+...+n = O(n^2). No depth/size bound exists anywhere in the file. The CVE-2026-27601 trampoline (line 20) deliberately moved traversal onto a heap `todo` array, removing the native call-stack ceiling that previously made deep inputs RangeError out early; deep inputs now run to completion and reach the quadratic path. _.isEqual is a public API (index.js:41) whose operands are caller/attacker-controlled data (e.g. parsed JSON), and superlinear work from modest-size structured input is algorithmic complexity, which the exclusion rules keep in-scope rather than excluding as volumetric DoS.

Severity derived LOW: this is a pure CPU-burn algorithmic-complexity DoS (no memory corruption, no data exposure) gated behind 3+ preconditions, most critically that an application both calls _.isEqual on attacker-supplied data AND the comparison reaches the deep matching-structure path rather than early-returning. The 3+ precondition count and the absence of a demonstrated end-to-end path both land on LOW; the lower-of-columns rule confirms LOW even though access could be unauthenticated-remote. Scanner's LOW is well-justified (alignment +3). needs_manual_test: a human PoC should confirm whether realistic depths produce a wall-clock DoS before the quadratic factor stops mattering.
findings.owner_hint: top committer: Julian Gonggrijp (13/14 recent commits on modules/isEqual.js); no CODEOWNERS entry

findings.title: Template body compiled into new Function -- RCE by design on untrusted template string
findings.rationale: FALSE_POSITIVE by 3/3 verifiers (exclusion rule 3, intentional_behavior). The new Function sink at template.js:87 is driven entirely by the template SOURCE STRING (text); runtime data is safely escaped as string literals at template.js:53,57-59 and never reaches the sink as code. SECURITY.md:27-45 explicitly documents this as an intended eval-like contract ('allowed by design... the main feature... responsibility of the user not to pass untrusted input'). The one real injection lever (settings.variable, CVE-2021-23358) is separately guarded by bareIdentifier at template.js:32,72. In a Library/SDK where the caller is the trust boundary, supplying an untrusted template string is caller misuse, not an actionable library defect.

findings.title: _.escape covers only 6 HTML chars; unsafe for unquoted-attr/URL/JS/CSS contexts
findings.rationale: FALSE_POSITIVE by 3/3 verifiers (exclusion rule 3, intentional_behavior). _.escape (escape.js:5, _escapeMap.js:2-9) is a standard HTML-entity escaper documented for HTML interpolation (index.html:2653-2654); it escapes & < > " ' and backtick, fully covering HTML element body and both quoted-attribute contexts. The / omission is a deliberate, documented decision (index.html:3744 'no longer escapes /'). The claimed exploit (>) only works because the caller authored an UNQUOTED attribute; the library never constructs URL/unquoted-attribute/JS/CSS contexts itself (template.js:57 substitutes escaped output into the caller's own literal). Context-specific encoding is intended-design caller responsibility, not a library bug.

findings.title: extend/extendOwn/defaults copy attacker-controlled __proto__ key onto destination
findings.rationale: FALSE_POSITIVE by 3/3 verifiers (misread_code; exclusion rules 12/3). At _createAssigner.js:13 the assignment obj[key]=source[key] is a single-level shallow copy with no recursive descent (the only consumers are extend.js:5, extendOwn.js:7, defaults.js:5, none of which recurse). For key==='__proto__', obj['__proto__']=value invokes the __proto__ accessor setter / [[SetPrototypeOf]], which rebinds ONLY the single destination object's [[Prototype]]; it does NOT write properties onto the shared global Object.prototype. Classic prototype pollution requires a recursive merge that writes THROUGH __proto__ (dest.__proto__.x = y), which underscore's shallow assigners never do. The scanner conflated local prototype-rebind of one caller-owned object with global pollution; the residual effect is a low-impact, object-local quirk.

findings.title: bareIdentifier guard on settings.variable -- no bypass found
findings.rationale: FALSE_POSITIVE by 3/3 verifiers (already_handled; exclusion rules 8/13). The new Function sink at template.js:87 and template.source at :98 are guarded at template.js:72 by bareIdentifier (template.js:32) /^\s*(\w|\$)+\s*$/, the CVE-2021-23358 fix. The anchored regex (no m flag) admits only [\w$] runs framed by optional whitespace, excluding every code-bearing character ()/,/=/{/[/;) needed for default-value or destructuring breakout; interior newlines break the match because only the outer \s* allows whitespace, and the guard and sink both coerce via String() so no malicious-toString TOCTOU split exists. No bypass found, and settings.variable is caller/template-author-controlled at the same trust level as the template body (which already compiles to arbitrary code by design), so even a hypothetical bypass grants no privilege the caller lacks.

findings.title: property/get path traversal can read prototype-chain members via attacker path
findings.rationale: FALSE_POSITIVE by 3/3 verifiers (intentional_behavior / not_actionable; exclusion rules 13/3). _deepGet.js:6 performs read-only key-path traversal (obj=obj[path[i]]) with no assignment, so there is no prototype-pollution write primitive. Reading obj.__proto__ / obj.constructor.prototype returns process-global intrinsic objects already reachable by any code holding obj (get.js:10, property.js:9, toPath.js:7); no confidential, caller-private data crosses a trust boundary. The path is developer-chosen (caller is the trust boundary); bracket-access prototype traversal is the documented intended semantic of a deep getter. At most a missing own-property guard with no concrete exploit.

findings.title: _.invoke resolves a method by an attacker path and applies it
findings.rationale: FALSE_POSITIVE by 3/3 verifiers (intentional_behavior; exclusion rules 3/8). invoke.js:24-26 does method=context[path]; method.apply(context,args) -- it dereferences an existing object member by a caller-chosen name and calls it. This is the documented contract of _.invoke (invoke.js:7-8 'Invoke a method on every item'), and the caller is the trust boundary that supplies path/args. path is fixed across all items (line 15), so attacker-controlled list DATA cannot redirect it. The sink is a property lookup + apply, never string-to-code eval, so a caller-chosen string is never turned into attacker-authored code -- no code injection / ACE is reachable. Resolving an inherited member is no escalation beyond the caller's chosen action.

findings.title: _flatten trampoline has no cycle detection; self-referential array loops unbounded
findings.rationale: FALSE_POSITIVE by 3/3 verifiers (implausible_trigger; exclusion rule 8). The infinite loop is real -- with default depth=Infinity (_flatten.js:8) the guard at line 23 never fires, so a self-referential array re-pushes frames at lines 25-30 with no cycle detection until OOM. However the trigger requires a caller-built cyclic array (a.push(a)); JSON and any deserialization cannot produce cycles, and underscore never assembles cycles from untrusted data (callers: flatten.js, union.js, difference.js, pick.js, omit.js, bindAll.js all pass caller-supplied arrays). In a Library/SDK where the caller is the trust boundary, this is a trusted-input-only condition; the realistic untrusted vector (deep-nested JSON -> stack overflow) is already defended by the trampoline (lines 9-11, CVE-2026-27601). Not a regression.
